reasoning: The informal statement claims the SERIES $\sum 1/(k(\log k)^p)$ diverges for $p \leq 1$, which is a well-known true result (Cauchy condensation). The CORRECTED formalization uses `¬ Summable f`, correctly capturing series divergence. The ORIGINAL formalization uses `¬ ∃ l, Tendsto f atTop (𝓝 l)`, which asserts the SEQUENCE has no limit at infinity — a completely different property. For this specific sequence f(k) = 1/(k(log k)^p) with p ≤ 1, f(k) actually tends to 0 as k → ∞ (in all cases: p > 0, p = 0, p < 0), so the sequence DOES converge, making the ORIGINAL statement provably false. Sequence convergence and series convergence are logically independent properties, so the two formalizations are incomparable. The error is a misunderstanding of what the ∑ symbol in the informal statement refers to (the series, not the sequence).
